text:
Now here, and these, and hence in peace, all yours, O Flag!  
And here, and hence, for you, O universal Muse—and you for them!  
And here, and hence, O Union, all the labor and the laborers yours!  
No one apart from you—henceforth only one, we and you,  
(For the blood of the children—what is it, if not the mother’s blood?  
And lives and works—what are they in the end but the roads to  
faith and death?)
source_text:
Now here and these and hence in peace, all thine O Flag!
And here and hence for thee, O universal Muse! and thou for them!
And here and hence O Union, all the work and workmen thine!
None separate from thee--henceforth One only, we and thou,
(For the blood of the children, what is it, only the blood maternal?
And lives and works, what are they all at last, except the roads to
faith and death?)

text:
How could I deem the solitary night fair,
though your tender wishes lend it wings?
Let it not be spoken, thought, or known—
and then it shall become good night.
source_text:
How can I call the lone night good,
Though thy sweet wishes wing its flight?
Be it not said, thought, understood—
Then it will be—GOOD night.

text:
Weary of such tender kisses,  
they consent to meet  
when hush-bound sleep  
rolls over heaven’s depth,  
and the tired, wandering souls still weep.
source_text:
Tired with kisses sweet,
They agree to meet
When the silent sleep
Waves o’er heaven’s deep,
And the weary tired wanderers weep.

text:
I sing to pass the waiting,
My bonnet only to fasten,
And close the door upon my house;
No other task is mine,
source_text:
I sing to use the waiting,
My bonnet but to tie,
And shut the door unto my house;
No more to do have I,

text:
SEMICHORUS 1:
Endless, and swift, and wild, and unbound,
With the Spirits that shape a new earth and sea,
And a heaven where heaven had never been found;
source_text:
SEMICHORUS 1:
Ceaseless, and rapid, and fierce, and free,
With the Spirits which build a new earth and sea,
And a heaven where yet heaven could never be;

text:
So sang a small lump of earth,
Worn beneath the cattle’s tread,
Yet a pebble from the stream
Trilled these fitting lines instead:
source_text:
So sung a little clod of clay,
Trodden with the cattle’s feet,
But a pebble of the brook
Warbled out these metres meet:

text:
Swift turns in that struggle—more than one setback,  
and more than one reversal, a dark, untamed whirl;  
now the Snake wound round his enemy’s neck  
and bound him fast in iron, unyielding coils,  
until the Eagle, spent with pain and strain,  
relaxed his mighty flight and, near the sea,  
beat his wings in weary strokes, no hope remaining  
of frustrating now the foe above him,  
who lifted high his red, aflame crest, bright with triumph.
source_text:
Swift chances in that combat—many a check,
And many a change, a dark and wild turmoil;
Sometimes the Snake around his enemy’s neck
Locked in stiff rings his adamantine coil,
Until the Eagle, faint with pain and toil,
Remitted his strong flight, and near the sea
Languidly fluttered, hopeless so to foil
His adversary, who then reared on high
His red and burning crest, radiant with victory.

text:
Not only cast your subtle radiance and your strength for these,
but ready the later afternoon of my own being—ready my longening shadows,
ready my nights, alive with stars.
source_text:
Nor only launch thy subtle dazzle and thy strength for these,
Prepare the later afternoon of me myself--prepare my lengthening shadows,
Prepare my starry nights.

text:
“I am meant to brighten the earth,
While the beetle circles in his girth:
Follow now the beetle’s drone;
Little wanderer, speed thee home!”
source_text:
‘I am set to light the ground,
While the beetle goes his round:
Follow now the beetle’s hum;
Little wanderer, hie thee home!’

text:
With heedless foot I wandered on,  
My heart was glad in nature’s mirth;  
When, pondering in a lonely lawn,  
I chanced to see a maid of worth:  
Her gaze was like the dawn’s own birth,  
Her mien like spring’s soft, blooming guile:  
And Perfection, as it wandered forth,  
Said, “Mark the lass o’ Ballochmyle!”
source_text:
With careless step I onward stray’d,
My heart rejoic’d in nature’s joy,
When, musing in a lonely glade,
A maiden fair I chanc’d to spy:
Her look was like the morning’s eye,
Her air like nature’s vernal smile:
Perfection whisper’d, passing by,
“Behold the lass o’ Ballochmyle!”

text:
For you, no child of stall and byre,  
Who sweetly wake the Scottish lyre,  
My thanks for all your line:  
The checkered plaid you kindly spare  
Should, by my hand, be cherished fair;  
It would delight me fine.  
I’d rather boast of fortune’s grace,  
With softly hanging over me,  
Than any ermine in its place,  
Or purple meant for majesty.  
Farewell then, long be well then,  
And plenty fill your day;  
May losses and crosses  
Ne’er at your hallan stay!
source_text:
For you, no bred to barn and byre,
Wha sweetly tune the Scottish lyre,
Thanks to you for your line:
The marled plaid ye kindly spare,
By me should gratefully be ware;
’Twad please me to the nine.
I’d be mair vauntie o’ my hap,
Douce hingin owre my curple,
Than ony ermine ever lap,
Or proud imperial purple.
Farewell then, lang hale then,
An’ plenty be your fa;
May losses and crosses
Ne’er at your hallan ca’!

text:
Already a casual kind, rising quietly, coming into view on the streets,  
People’s mouths reserve praise for doers, lovers, pleasers, those who know with certainty,  
Soon there will be no more priests, I say their labor is finished,  
Death knows no emergencies here, yet life is one continual emergency here,  
Are your body, your days, your habits excellent? after death you shall be excellent,  
Justice, health, self-respect, sweep the path aside with force that cannot be resisted;  
How dare you set anything above a man?
source_text:
Already a nonchalant breed, silently emerging, appears on the streets,
People’s lips salute only doers, lovers, satisfiers, positive knowers,
There will shortly be no more priests, I say their work is done,
Death is without emergencies here, but life is perpetual emergencies here,
Are your body, days, manners, superb? after death you shall be superb,
Justice, health, self-esteem, clear the way with irresistible power;
How dare you place any thing before a man?

text:
The revised 1818 version of this poem is presented here, as the one  
Shelley himself brought into print. To restore “The Revolt of Islam”  
to the form of “Laon and Cythna,” the reader must make  
the following changes in the text. At the close of the “Preface,”  
add:—
source_text:
The revised text (1818) of this poem is given here, as being that which
Shelley actually published. In order to reconvert the text of “The
Revolt of Islam” into that of “Laon and Cythna”, the reader must make
the following alterations in the text. At the end of the “Preface”
add:—

text:
And a few, like the ones we know,
doomed—though only God knows why—
to believe their minds were made
to turn this ugly hell to heaven;
in that faith, they live and die.
source_text:
And some few, like we know who,
Damned—but God alone knows why—
To believe their minds are given
To make this ugly Hell a Heaven;
In which faith they live and die.

text:
All thought God’s angel had arrived to sweep  
the lingering guilty down to fiery grave;  
the tyrant from his throne in terror leapt—  
her innocence, his child, from fear, still saved.  
Afraid of faith they only seemed to claim,  
each priestly slave knelt low to beg his grace,  
the one they served with blood and called by name;  
and like a great wave drawing back to sea,  
the multitude was pulled beneath loud panic’s tide.
source_text:
All thought it was God’s Angel come to sweep
The lingering guilty to their fiery grave;
The Tyrant from his throne in dread did leap,—
Her innocence his child from fear did save;
Scared by the faith they feigned, each priestly slave
Knelt for his mercy whom they served with blood,
And, like the refluence of a mighty wave
Sucked into the loud sea, the multitude
With crushing panic, fled in terror’s altered mood.

text:
O these themes—these equalities! O sacred mean!
Songs beneath the sun, arriving as now, or at noon, or in dusk,
Melodies moving through the years, and at last coming here,
I meet your wild, many-voiced harmonies, add my own, and
gladly send them onward.
source_text:
O such themes--equalities! O divine average!
Warblings under the sun, usher’d as now, or at noon, or setting,
Strains musical flowing through ages, now reaching hither,
I take to your reckless and composite chords, add to them, and
cheerfully pass them forward.

text:
The climbing moon began to shine  
Beyond the far Cumnock hills to pine:  
To count her horns, with all my power,  
I set myself;  
Yet whether she wore three or four,  
I could not tell.
source_text:
The rising moon began to glowre
The distant Cumnock hills out-owre:
To count her horns, wi’ a my pow’r,
I set mysel’;
But whether she had three or four,
I cou’d na tell.

text:
At least there still remains to pray, to pray.
O Jesus, in the air
I do not know which room is thine, --
I knock at every stair.
source_text:
At least to pray is left, is left.
O Jesus! in the air
I know not which thy chamber is, --
I 'm knocking everywhere.

text:
My Lord Archbishop,  
Do as you will, and all you are able, in this.  
Your earthly, as your heavenly, king  
Grants you wide power in his restless realm.  
Yet we need money, and my heart warns me  
That for so grand an undertaking, still,  
We are not supplied.
source_text:
KING:
My Lord Archbishop,
Do what thou wilt and what thou canst in this.
Thy earthly even as thy heavenly King
Gives thee large power in his unquiet realm.
But we want money, and my mind misgives me
That for so great an enterprise, as yet,
We are unfurnished.

text:
Beyond lay distant glades and the adjoining lawn,
And through the deep green throng
The pale sun flickered like the dawn
Beneath a cloud of specks.
source_text:
There lay far glades and neighbouring lawn,
And through the dark green crowd
The white sun twinkling like the dawn
Under a speckled cloud.

text:
Dost thou know the radiant joys of youth?  
The joy of dear companions, of lively speech and faces bright with laughter?  
The joy of a day ablaze with light, the joy of broad-lunged games?  
The joy of sweet music, the joy of the lit ballroom and the dancers?  
The joy of a generous feast, of stout revelry and drinking?
source_text:
Knowist thou the excellent joys of youth?
Joys of the dear companions and of the merry word and laughing face?
Joy of the glad light-beaming day, joy of the wide-breath’d games?
Joy of sweet music, joy of the lighted ball-room and the dancers?
Joy of the plenteous dinner, strong carouse and drinking?

text:
[Footnote 2: Another preacher, a like-loved one among the few,  
who then was suffering ill health. For him, see also “The Ordination,”  
stanza ix.—R.B.]
source_text:
[Footnote 2: Another preacher, an equal favourite with the few,
who was at that time ailing. For him see also “The Ordination,”
stanza ix.—R.B.]

text:
Has old Kilmarnock glimpsed the deil?
Or great Mackinlay turned his heel?
Or Robertson once more grown well,
To preach and read?
“No worse than all!” cries every chiel,
“Tam Samson’s dead!”
source_text:
Has auld Kilmarnock seen the deil?
Or great Mackinlay thrawn his heel?
Or Robertson again grown weel,
To preach an’ read?
“Na’ waur than a’!” cries ilka chiel,
“Tam Samson’s dead!”

text:
I woke to find it stirring first,  
I rose, and it came after me;  
I tried to cast it off in crowds,  
To drown it in the sea,
source_text:
I waked, to find it first awake,
I rose, -- it followed me;
I tried to drop it in the crowd,
To lose it in the sea,

text:
In every cry that comes from every man,  
In every infant’s cry, trembling with fear,  
In every voice, in every spoken ban,  
I hear the mind-forged shackles ringing clear:
source_text:
In every cry of every man,
In every infant’s cry of fear,
In every voice, in every ban,
The mind-forged manacles I hear:

text:
Open the flood, and you will see it plain,  
Wave upon wave, all kept for you;  
Scarlet test! doubtful Thomas,  
Now do you doubt your bird was true?
source_text:
Loose the flood, you shall find it patent,
Gush after gush, reserved for you;
Scarlet experiment! sceptic Thomas,
Now, do you doubt that your bird was true?

text:
Heavy as the death-birds on Erin’s shore,  
that gorged themselves on her dearest blood,  
and circled Castlereagh  
when they tore the Patriot’s heart from HIS grasp,  
after it had been wrenched from his widow’s mad clasp,  
and vanished with the first light of day.
source_text:
Fat—as the Death-birds on Erin’s shore,
That glutted themselves in her dearest gore,
And flitted round Castlereagh,
When they snatched the Patriot’s heart, that HIS grasp
Had torn from its widow’s maniac clasp,
—And fled at the dawn of day.

text:
Chorus—O good ale comes and good ale goes;
Good ale makes me sell my hose,
Sell my hose, and pawn my shoes—
Good ale keeps my heart above!
source_text:
Chorus—O gude ale comes and gude ale goes;
Gude ale gars me sell my hose,
Sell my hose, and pawn my shoon—
Gude ale keeps my heart aboon!
